title_of_original: State aid SA.34155 (2013/N) (ex 2011/PN) – Germany Regional law on the compensation of school bus transport in the Land Rhineland-Palatinate
case_description: [1] In 2016, the Austrian province of Styria informed the European Commission about a new regional compensation scheme for school and student transport. [2] This scheme replaced an earlier federal mechanism that had been based on Section 24a of the Austrian Public Transport Act (ÖPNVG). [3] The new arrangement was set out in a specific regional law on public service compensation and [4] backed by an implementing regulation issued by the Styrian government. [5] The scheme places a requirement on all bus and tram operators in Styria. [6] These operators are required to provide regular, reliable, and non-discriminatory transport services on designated routes within the province, [7] with a specific obligation to offer discounted fares of at least 20 percent below the standard adult fare to eligible categories: [8] pupils enrolled in compulsory education, full-time students up to age 26, and registered apprentices under Austrian vocational training law. [9] The goal is to make transport to education more affordable, help families with children, and support environmentally friendly public transport instead of private cars. [10] In return for this obligation, the undertakings receive compensation from the regional budget. [11] This is paid out upon application by the Landesverkehrsabteilung Steiermark, a transport authority of the Land. [12] The basic compensation equals the difference between the reduced fare and the normal fare. [13] For special products like the RegioMobil card (used in the Graz area) and the semester ticket for university students, [14] operators also receive an extra 35 percent of the ticket revenue. [15] This extra amount reflects the fact that these tickets rely on solidarity financing and have a flat-rate pricing model. [16] To prevent overcompensation, the scheme includes two limits. [17] First, compensation is capped at the fare difference or the extra share of ticket revenue. [18] Second, a further cap is based on the actual net costs of fulfilling the obligation. [19] This is calculated using a formula that follows the rules in Regulation 1370/2007: eligible costs plus a reasonable profit minus any income. [20] The regulation also requires operators to follow Austrian cost accounting standards (ÖKRG), [21] which only allow for efficient and necessary costs. [22] Revenues must include not just ticket sales, but also any advertising income, subsidy payments, or revenue-sharing deals. [23] Operators are required to keep separate accounts for the services covered by the scheme. [24] They must submit certified reports from auditors each year, [25] and the granting authority carries out annual checks.  [26] The scheme is funded from Styria’s public budget, with around 39.9 million euros allocated each year. [27] It took effect on 1. September 2017 and is set to run until the end of 2027. [28] There is also a review clause: if total compensation in 2020 is more than 3 percent higher than in 2017, the scheme must be reassessed.
